metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 8: Scientific Figures -- a 5-step pipeline (Plan, Build, Compose, Validate, Export) powered by the figures plugin (5 skills + the figure-qa agent), with validate_fonts.py as the new mechanical defence

elements.content: # Agentic Research Course
Week 8: Scientific Figures

**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)

Course: [courses.osc.earth/agentic-research](https://courses.osc.earth/agentic-research/) | Discord: [discord.gg/5dWJCUmUww](https://discord.gg/5dWJCUmUww) | Recording: published to YouTube within 48 h

slides.speakerNotes:
- Welcome back

- Last week: a full IMRAD manuscript, GitHub-native end-to-end, with an Overleaf round-trip and the comment-merge manual pass

- Today: the figures stage we used but did not teach. The `figures` plugin expanded between last week and this week from 3 skills to 5 skills + a QA agent

- The new defence introduced today: `validate_fonts.py` -- a deterministic check on effective font sizes across the transform stack. Fixes the journal-rejection failure mode the old workflow had

- Format: ~30 min presentation, ~5 min live demo of the HBN practicum algorithm figure, 15 min Q&A

elements.content: ## Where We Are

items.text: **Weeks 1-4 -- Git, Claude Code, project management, CI/CD.** The safety net and the agent.
**Week 5 -- Lit review.** Cite-the-card discipline. The corpus that fed Weeks 6 and 7.
**Week 6 -- Grant proposals.** Specific Aims = 1-page lit review with deliverables; `grant-figure-qa` was the first figure-QA agent.
**Week 7 -- Manuscripts.** Stage 3 of the IMRAD pipeline was figures-as-workflow. Today is figures-as-craft.
**Today.** The 5-step figures pipeline. Plan, Build, Compose, Validate, Export.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- Recap of weeks 1-7, paired by theme

- The Week 6 callout (`grant-figure-qa`) sets up today's unified `figure-qa` agent

- The Week 7 callout sets up the figures-as-craft framing for today

- Land the closing bullet: 'figures-as-craft', the discipline the manuscript pipeline assumes but does not teach

elements.content: ## Why the Old Workflow Broke

elements.alt: Two figure renderings side by side. Left side: a Nature 1-column panel target with crisp 7 pt axis labels. Right side: the same panel rendered through react-pdf with the y-axis label Frequency (Hz) highlighted in red at 4.5 pt, and a hand-drawn diagonal slash across the panel. A failure-modes strip across the bottom lists two failures: composition did not respect physical size; fonts shrank below readable thresholds.

elements.content: **Two structural failures.** Flexbox shifted dimensions during render; engine scaled the whole figure, pushing axis labels under 4 pt. **Journal-reject territory.**

slides.speakerNotes:
- This is the motivation slide. Land both failures aloud

- Failure 1: composition did not respect physical size. The flexbox layout engine treated mm as soft constraints; panels drifted by a fraction of a mm during render. Over a 2-column figure, that drifted into misaligned panel labels

- Failure 2: fonts shrank. When a panel's content overflowed its allotted space, the engine scaled the entire figure uniformly. Axis labels that started at 7 pt landed at 4-4.5 pt -- below Nature's 5 pt minimum

- The right-hand panel's red-highlighted label is the failure made visible. This is what triggered the rewrite

elements.content: ## Reframe -- Figures Are a 5-Step Pipeline

elements.alt: Left side: an old one-shot generator labelled react-pdf, with a single arrow into a garbage-output warning box. Right side: a 5-step pipeline labelled Plan, Build, Compose, Validate, Export, with a boomerang arrow from Validate back to Build. Annotations call out the shift from one-shot to pipeline.

elements.content: Same shape as Weeks 5-7. **A figure is a managed pipeline, not a single prompt.** Every step has a mechanical defence.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Land the reframe aloud: figures-as-pipeline, not figures-as-prompt

- The shape carries from prior weeks. Trainees who learned the lit-review or manuscript pipelines recognise it instantly

- The boomerang is the new visual: when the font validator at Step 4 flags an issue, the failed panel cycles back to Step 2 (Build) for rescaling or re-export

elements.content: ## The 5-Step scientific-figure Pipeline

elements.alt: Five-step horizontal pipeline: Plan (journal size, panel grid, palette); Build (matplotlib/seaborn per panel; optional icons and substrates); Compose (svgutils at exact mm coordinates; text preserved); Validate (per-element pt vs journal minimum; rescale panel if below minimum); Export (Inkscape if present; cairosvg otherwise). A boomerang arrow loops from Validate back to Build.

elements.content: Order enforced. Composing before planning produces dimension drift; exporting before validating produces journal rejections. **The validator is the new mechanical defence.**

slides.speakerNotes:
- The hero diagram for this week. Read each stage aloud

- Plan -> Build -> Compose -> Validate -> Export

- The boomerang from Validate back to Build is the rigor signal; first-pass convergence means the validator did not actually run or the figure has no text to measure

- The validator is what makes this pipeline different from every other figure workflow I have used

elements.content: ## Step 1 -- Pick the Journal First

elements.alt: A 4-row table headed Pick the journal first. Columns are Journal, 1-column width, 2-column width, Min body font, Notes. Rows: Nature 89 mm 183 mm 5 pt 8 pt panel labels; Science 55 mm 120 mm 6 pt Myriad/Helvetica preferred; Cell 85 mm 174 mm 6 pt max 225 mm tall; PNAS 87 mm 180 mm 6 pt no label below 2 mm tall.

elements.content: Width sets the canvas. **Min body font is the validator's threshold.** Pick early so the validator can warn early.

slides.speakerNotes:
- This is the slide that prevents the most common figure failure: building at the wrong width

- Nature 89/183, Science 55/120, Cell 85/174, PNAS 87/180. Memorise the one you submit to most often

- Min body font is what the validator enforces. Nature 5 pt, the other three at 6 pt

- The full table for ~20 journals lives in `references/journal-specs.md`

elements.content: ## Step 1 -- Colorblind-Safe Palettes

elements.alt: Five palette rows with named swatches and hex codes. Wong (8 swatches): black, orange, sky blue, bluish green, yellow, blue, vermilion, reddish purple. Okabe-Ito (8 swatches): same family, lab-calibrated. Tol bright (7 swatches): blue, red, green, yellow, cyan, purple, grey. Viridis sequential (8 gradient steps). Crameri batlow (8 gradient steps).

elements.content: Reuse one palette across all panels. The theme bible (`theme.json`) keeps an icon set + plot panels + substrates in the same colour language.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Five palettes the plugin ships with: Wong, Okabe-Ito (both categorical, colour-blind safe), Tol bright (categorical, high contrast), viridis (sequential, perceptually uniform), Crameri batlow (sequential, designed for scientific data)

- The full catalogue (~15 palettes) lives in `references/color-palettes.md`

- The theme bible (`theme.json`) is what stops palette drift across the 8 panels of a multi-panel figure

elements.content: ## The figures Plugin -- 5 Skills + 1 Agent

elements.alt: Centre card: /figures:scientific-figure (the composer). Four element-builder cards around it: /figures:plot-styling (matplotlib, seaborn); /figures:svg-figure (schematics, arrows); /figures:transparent-icons (Codex CLI image_gen, transparency post-process); /figures:ai-full-figure (substrate + overlay). Above the composer: a /figures:figure-qa agent card with arrows down to the composer indicating it runs on every figure produced.

elements.content: The composer is the sink. The element builders feed it. **The QA agent runs on every figure** (programmatic checks plus a VLM aesthetic pass).

slides.speakerNotes:
- The plugin map slide. The plugin expanded between Weeks 7 and 8 from 3 skills to 5 + the QA agent

- Composer at the centre: `/figures:scientific-figure`. Everything feeds it

- Four element builders: `plot-styling` (data), `svg-figure` (schematics), `transparent-icons` (icons), `ai-full-figure` (pictorial substrates)

- QA agent on top, runs on every figure unless explicitly opted out

- Next four slides: one per element-builder skill

elements.content: ## /figures:plot-styling -- SciencePlots + SVG-with-text

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left: a Python code snippet showing import matplotlib.pyplot as plt, import scienceplots, plt.style.use(['science', 'nature', 'no-latex']), then fig.savefig('panel.svg', transparent=True, bbox_inches='tight'). The style.use line and the savefig kwargs are highlighted. Right: a rendered Nature-style line plot with axis labels annotated at 7 pt and a colorblind-safe Wong palette badge.

elements.content: **Save as SVG, transparent, tight bbox, embedded text.** The validator inspects every `<text>` element; rasterised PNG breaks the check.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The library decision tree: matplotlib for control, seaborn for stats, plotnine for ggplot in Python, plotly for HTML supplements

- SciencePlots styles do 80% of the work: `science` (base), `nature` / `ieee` / `vibrant` / `bright`

- `no-latex` is non-optional unless you have system LaTeX installed AND want path-rendered text (which breaks font validation)

- Export discipline: SVG, transparent, tight bbox, embedded text. The validator depends on text-as-text

elements.content: ## /figures:svg-figure -- Schematics with Constraints

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left: an SVG code snippet showing a rect at x=10 y=10 width=30 height=14, a text element at x=25 y=17 with text-anchor=middle dominant-baseline=middle reading Cortex, and a line with marker-end pointing at a target rect. Comments call out text centred in box and arrow tip at target edge. Right: the rendered schematic showing the Cortex box, the arrow, and a labelled target box.

elements.content: Text **inside** box bounds. Arrows end at **target edge** (`refX` near tip). Lines pass under shapes via **document order** -- avoid `z-index`.

slides.speakerNotes:
- For schematics: boxes, arrows, labels. Use when the figure is structural rather than data

- Three constraints the skill enforces and `figure-qa` later checks: text aligned to box bounds; arrows ending at target edge; lines passing under shapes by document order

- The viewBox convention: `width=89mm height=60mm viewBox=0 0 89 60` so user units equal mm. A `<text font-size=9>` is 9 pt; a `<rect width=20>` is 20 mm

- The skill ships authoring patterns; the user can hand-write SVG or use svgutils.transform for programmatic schematics

elements.content: ## /figures:transparent-icons -- Codex First, API Fallback

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left: a shell command uv run --with openai --with pillow python scripts/generate_icon.py --template brain-eeg -o brain_eeg.png --transparent, with the --template and --transparent flags highlighted. A small backend toggle note: Codex CLI image_gen (preferred) or OpenAI Images API (fallback). Right: three rendered icons in a row (brain with EEG electrodes, neuron, EEG cap) on a checkerboard transparency background, all in a matching Wong palette.

elements.content: **No API key required** when `codex login` is configured. Transparency post-process: `threshold` (fast) or `birefnet` (cleaner edges, opt-in).

slides.speakerNotes:
- Two backends, auto-selected: Codex CLI (preferred -- uses local `codex login`), OpenAI Images API (fallback -- requires `OPENAI_API_KEY`)

- Why two: many researchers have a ChatGPT subscription and `codex login` but no separate API key

- Transparency is a post-process because gpt-image-2 (April 2026) does not accept `background=transparent`. Two methods: `threshold` (Pillow, fast, default) or `birefnet` (rembg + BiRefNet, opt-in, cleaner edges, ~400 MB one-time model download)

- The theme bible (`theme.json`) keeps an icon set consistent across the figure

elements.content: ## /figures:ai-full-figure -- Substrate Only, Overlay Programmatic

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left: a raw substrate showing a watercolour-style human brain on a clean white background with no text, no labels, no arrows. Right: the same substrate with three programmatic labels (Cortex, Cerebellum, Brainstem), two arrows pointing at anatomical landmarks, and a 10 mm scale bar overlaid as a separate SVG layer. A bottom strip lists the hard ceiling: no axis numerals, no equations, no multi-arrow flowcharts, no > 5 labeled elements.

elements.content: AI cannot reliably render text inside the image. **Substrate-only generation + programmatic overlay** is the pattern.

slides.speakerNotes:
- For pictorial scenes: brain renderings, microscope setups, anatomical scenes, lab apparatus, graphical-abstract substrates

- The hard ceiling: AI cannot reliably render axis numerals, equations, multi-arrow flowcharts, or more than 5 labelled elements. Labels longer than 1-2 words drift in position

- The pattern: prompt for the substrate with no text, no labels, no arrows; overlay the labels / arrows / scale bars as a separate SVG layer; ship the combined SVG

- The theme bible is shared with `transparent-icons` so substrate palette + icon palette match

elements.content: ## Step 3 -- /figures:scientific-figure (svgutils, Exact mm)

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left: a panels-config.json with width_mm 183, height_mm 120, journal nature, and three panels A (spectrum.svg at x_mm 0 y_mm 0 scale 0.5), B (topomap.svg at x_mm 92 y_mm 0 scale 0.5), C (timecourse.svg at x_mm 0 y_mm 60 scale 1.0 width_mm 183). Right: the composed figure at 183 mm wide showing two top panels labelled A and B and one wide bottom panel labelled C, with panel labels in 12 pt bold sans-serif.

elements.content: Panels at **exact mm coordinates**, not flexbox. **Text preserved as `<text>` elements** so the validator can inspect every label before export.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The composer is built around svgutils (`uv run --with svgutils`)

- Two ways to compose: the helper (`scripts/compose.py panels-config.json -o figure.svg`) for the 80% case, or direct `svgutils.compose.Figure` for full control

- The mm coords are not approximations; svgutils preserves the `viewBox` and `transform` stack so each panel lands at the exact x_mm/y_mm requested

- Text remains text. This is the property the validator depends on -- no rasterisation, no path conversion at compose time

elements.content: ## Step 4 -- validate_fonts.py (the New Defence)

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left: a JSON output block showing svg figure.svg, journal nature, minimum_pt 5.0, checked_count 47, issue_count 1, with one issue object highlighted: text 'Frequency (Hz)', specified_pt 9.0, effective_pt 4.5, scale_x 0.5, minimum_pt 5.0. The effective_pt 4.5 is in red. Right: the same figure after the panel scale is increased from 0.5 to 0.6, with the previously flagged label now annotated at 5.4 pt and a green check mark.

elements.content: **Walks the transform stack** to compute effective pt for every `<text>` and `<tspan>`. Remedies: rescale panel up, increase source font, or upgrade canvas width.

slides.speakerNotes:
- This is the slide trainees will quote

- The validator parses every `<text>` and `<tspan>` with a `font-size`, walks the accumulated transform stack to compute the effective pt at final physical dimensions, and reports anything below the journal minimum

- The JSON output: `effective_pt: 4.5` against `minimum_pt: 5.0` for Nature. Caught before export

- Three remedies: rescale the offending panel up (and others down) instead of accepting small text; increase the source plot's font size so even at panel scale 0.5 it passes; upgrade to a wider canvas (1-col -> 1.5-col)

- This is `cite-the-card`'s equivalent for figures: a deterministic check that converts subjective into objective

elements.content: ## Step 5 -- Export (Inkscape vs cairosvg)

elements.alt: Two-column slide. Left card: Inkscape branch. SVG -> Inkscape -> PDF arrow with annotation text remains text, fonts subsetted, highest fidelity. A preferred badge on the card. Bottom note brew install inkscape (~200 MB one-time). Right card: cairosvg branch. SVG -> cairosvg -> PDF arrow with annotation text converted to paths when journal font absent. A fallback badge on the card. Bottom note no extra install required.

elements.content: `export.py` auto-detects Inkscape on `$PATH`. One-time `brew install inkscape` is worth the ~200 MB.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Two export backends, auto-selected

- Inkscape (preferred when present): text remains text, fonts subsetted, highest fidelity. One-time install

- cairosvg (fallback): works without Inkscape but produces lower-fidelity PDFs when journal-required fonts are not installed locally -- text gets converted to paths

- The script auto-detects Inkscape on `$PATH` and warns when it falls back

- This is the only slide today that asks the user to install something locally

elements.content: ## /figures:figure-qa -- Dispatch by Input Type

elements.alt: Three-column flow. Left column: four input-type cards (SVG, raster PNG/JPG/TIFF, plot script, composed-figure directory). Centre: a /figures:figure-qa agent diamond with a no-qa opt-out badge. Right column: three check-script cards (check_svg.py, check_raster.py, check_plot_script.py) and one VLM-judgment card (vision-language model aesthetic pass). Arrows route each input type to the right check script plus the VLM card.

elements.content: Detects input type, picks the right deterministic checks, **adds a VLM aesthetic pass on top**. Honours `no-qa` opt-out.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The QA agent dispatches on input type. Four supported types: SVG, raster, plot script (Python source), and composed-figure directory

- Each type maps to a specific deterministic-check script

- The VLM judgment pass runs on top of the deterministic checks, for every input type

- `no-qa` opt-out: pass `no-qa` in the prompt or args to skip QA entirely. Useful for fast iteration; we re-enable it before commit

elements.content: ## Deterministic Checks vs VLM Judgment

elements.alt: Two columns with a vertical divider. Left column titled Deterministic (programmatic) lists: hex codes, pt sizes, pixel positions, alpha values, bbox overlap, arrow tip distance, document-order z-order, palette membership. Right column titled VLM (judgment) lists: hierarchy reads clearly, layered correctly, panel labels positioned consistently, visual balance, callout placement, narrative arc across panels. A horizontal arrow at the top reads Strict separation: programmatic for ground truth, VLM for judgment.

elements.content: Programmatic checks own anything with **ground truth**. VLM owns **judgment**. **Never blur the line** -- the moment a VLM tries to judge an exact hex code, the QA loses its falsifiability.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The strict separation is the design discipline of the QA agent

- Deterministic checks own: anything you can write an exact assertion about. Hex codes are exactly equal. A pt size is below or above the minimum. A bbox overlaps or it does not

- VLM owns: judgment. 'Does the hierarchy read clearly' is not a deterministic question; it depends on visual context

- The agent's prompt explicitly forbids the VLM from re-judging anything the deterministic checks already covered. This is why the agent stays useful as the deterministic-check coverage grows

elements.content: ## Three Defences -- One per Phase

elements.alt: Three labelled shields. Shield 1 (Palette + theme consistency) covers the Build phase, carried from prior weeks. Shield 2 (Font validation -- validate_fonts.py) covers Compose -> Validate, marked with a NEW badge. Shield 3 (VLM aesthetic pass -- figure-qa) covers the QA phase, evolved from Week 6's grant-figure-qa.

elements.content: Stack-redundant by design. **None alone is sufficient.** The font validator is new this week; it is the slide trainees will quote.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The moral of the session. Three defences, deliberately redundant

- Palette + theme consistency carried from prior weeks (Week 5 cite-the-card extended to cite-the-figure; Week 6 paper-cards anchor every claim)

- Font validation is new this week. The thing that fixed the react-pdf failure mode

- VLM aesthetic pass evolved from Week 6's `grant-figure-qa`. Now unified across SVG / raster / script / composed-figure input types

- Land the punchline: 'A figure that passes without the validator running, or without the QA agent's aesthetic pass, is hiding what it found'

elements.content: ## Live Demo Roadmap -- HBN Algorithm Figure

elements.alt: Three live-action steps in a row with timing badges. Step 1 (1:30, /figures:svg-figure): draft the algorithm-flow panel -- BIDS load -> AMICA -> IClabel -> shot-aligned epoching -> ERSP -> cluster stats. Step 2 (1:30, /figures:plot-styling): build an example ERSP panel from example-ersp-data.csv at Nature 1-column width. Step 3 (2:00, /figures:scientific-figure + figure-qa): compose the two panels at 89 mm with A and B labels, run validate_fonts.py and the VLM aesthetic pass. An off-stage tail notes that if the validator flags a font, we walk the remedy.

elements.content: **We do not manufacture a finding.** If the validator flags a font, we walk the remedy (rescale the panel up). The VLM pass is part of the demo, not separate.

slides.speakerNotes:
- Three live actions, ~5 minutes total

- Step 1 (1:30): /figures:svg-figure drafts the 5-stage algorithm flow panel

- Step 2 (1:30): /figures:plot-styling builds the example ERSP panel from a stub data file

- Step 3 (2:00): /figures:scientific-figure composes the two panels at 89 mm, then figure-qa runs validate_fonts.py and the VLM aesthetic pass

- A likely natural finding given the source font sizes: the topomap colorbar label might land at 4.8 pt under Nature's 5 pt. If that surfaces, we rescale the topomap panel from 0.5 to 0.6 live

- Land the closing: 'We do not manufacture a finding. The validator does its job whether or not we hoped it would'

elements.content: ## What Today Gives You / What's Next

items.text: A **5-step figures pipeline** (Plan, Build, Compose, Validate, Export) inside the same repository as the manuscript.
A **font validator** (`validate_fonts.py`) that catches journal-rejection failures before export.
A **QA agent** (`/figures:figure-qa`) with deterministic checks + a VLM aesthetic pass.
**Today.** Publication-grade figures that ship next to the manuscript, reproducible from source, journal-compliant before submission.

elements.content:
**Next week -- neuroinformatics.** Brain Imaging Data Structure (BIDS), Hierarchical Event Descriptors (HED), and what the Methods section cites.

**Questions? Ask while the demo composes.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Land the takeaways one at a time

- Today: 5-step pipeline, font validator, QA agent

- Next week: neuroinformatics. BIDS, HED, Methods

- Land the closing line: 'Questions? Ask while the demo composes'
